unit.name: Subword modeling and the vocabulary problem
unit.description: Explains why fixed word vocabularies fail on typos, morphological variants, and new words, and motivates subword tokenization as the practical alternative used in modern NLP.
unit.summary: The lecture opens by showing why a finite word vocabulary is brittle: models trained with whole-word embeddings cannot gracefully handle typos like "laern," elongated spellings like "taaaasty," or productive morphology like "transformerify" [ts=184s] [ts=198s]. The instructor argues that mapping all unseen words to a single UNK token throws away too much information, and the problem becomes worse in morphologically rich languages such as Swahili, where a single verb can have hundreds of conjugated forms [ts=254s] [ts=311s]. The solution is subword modeling: start from characters, iteratively merge common adjacent substrings, and tokenize each word into the fewest large known pieces possible [ts=395s] [ts=638s]. This preserves open-vocabulary coverage, lets common words remain intact, and gives useful representations for novel forms such as segmented versions of "tasty" or "transformer" + "-ify" [ts=444s] [ts=549s]. The segment also clarifies that punctuation is typically retained and that the model treats full words and subwords uniformly as vocabulary indices [ts=665s] [ts=736s].

key_points.text: Using a single UNK token for unseen words loses substantial lexical information.
Morphologically rich languages make whole-word vocabularies especially inefficient because many related forms would get separate vectors.
Subword vocabularies can be learned by starting from characters and repeatedly merging frequent adjacent substrings.
At tokenization time, words are split into the largest available subwords so sequence length stays relatively small.
Modern NLP usually keeps punctuation and minimally preprocesses text so inputs stay close to real usage.
